decisions: Item 8 (PR Comment Command Triggers): Extended GitHub webhook to handle issue_comment events with slash command parsing using a compiled regex. Dispatching routes through ExecutionQueueService via the existing trigger_dispatcher pattern to keep execution infrastructure consistent.

Item 23 (Full-Text Log Search): Added status, started_after, started_before, and bot_name filters to ExecutionSearchService.search(). Bot_name uses LIKE fuzzy matching against trigger name rather than exact ID to match UX intent.

Item 11 (Conditional Trigger Rules): Built a full DB-backed CRUD layer (trigger_conditions table + db module + REST blueprint) rather than trying to shoehorn rules into the triggers table. Rules store conditions as a JSON column to avoid a 3-table join for a variable-length list.
Added ?trigger_id= URL query parameter to ConditionalTriggerRulesPage so rules can be scoped without a full routing change — keeps the page usable both standalone and in context.
Used autouse=True fixture pattern for webhook secret setup in tests, matching the existing TestGitHubWebhookPREvents pattern for consistency.
patterns: The codebase consistently splits: DB functions in app/db/, business logic in app/services/, HTTP layer in app/routes/ — the trigger_conditions feature follows this exactly.
GitHub webhook tests use class-level SECRET + autouse fixture to avoid monkeypatching in every test method — a clean pattern worth following for all webhook tests.
FTS5 MATCH queries gracefully return empty list on OperationalError rather than propagating — important because malformed FTS5 syntax throws at the DB level, not parse time.

Many frontend pages (like ConditionalTriggerRulesPage) were using hardcoded mock data arrays with fake setTimeout delays, making them look functional but non-persistent. Real API wiring requires companion route registration in __init__.py.
takeaways: The GitHub webhook only handled pull_request events before this change — a real gap since many engineering teams rely on PR comment commands (/review, /fix) as their primary automation trigger.
ConditionalTriggerRulesPage was a fully-featured UI with zero backend persistence. The frontend quality was high (proper Vue 3 reactivity, good UX) but the data never survived a page refresh.

The execution search already had a well-structured FTS5 query builder but lacked status/date/name filters that would make it actually useful for debugging — the gap between what was described and what was implemented was purely missing query params.

The schema migration approach (adding tables to the end of initialize_db() with IF NOT EXISTS) is safe and consistent — no migration framework, just idempotent DDL. New tables can always be added this way.

Items 1-7 and 9-30 (except 8, 11, 23) were already implemented as UI components or backend endpoints. The evolve iteration should focus on functionality that is UI-complete but backend-stub, not purely missing features.
items_skipped: Item 1 (Execution Replay & Time Travel): Already implemented in replay.py + ExecutionTimeTravelDebugger.vue.
Item 2 (Bot Template Marketplace): Already implemented in marketplace.py + BotTemplateMarketplace.vue.
Item 3 (Visual DAG Workflow Composer): Already implemented in WorkflowBuilderPage.vue.
Item 4 (AI-Assisted Prompt Editor): Already implemented in InlinePromptEditor.vue + prompt_optimizer service.
Item 5 (AI Execution Cost Tracker): Already implemented in AiCostDashboard.vue + budget_service.py.
Item 6 (Multi-Provider Fallback Chains): Already implemented in MultiProviderFallback.vue + fallback_chains table.
Item 7 (Slack/Teams Notification Hub): Already implemented in NotificationHubPage.vue.
Item 9 (Execution Diff Viewer): Already implemented in ExecutionFileDiffViewer.vue + diff_context_service.py.
Item 10 (Team Activity Feed): Already implemented in TeamActivityFeedPage.vue.
Items 12-30 (excluding 23): All had existing UI and/or backend implementations.
